name: pyabf

metadata.name: pyabf
classifiers: Topic :: Software Development :: Libraries :: Application Frameworks
Topic :: Software Development :: Libraries :: Python Modules
metadata.summary: Python library for reading files in Axon Binary Format (ABF)

documentation:
# pyABF: a pure-Python ABF file reader

**pyABF** provides a Python interface to electrophysiology files in the Axon Binary Format (ABF).
pyABF supports Python 3 and does not use obscure libraries (just numpy and matplotlib).
pyABF supports reading ABF1 and ABF2 files and can write ABF1 files.

```
.. class:: no-web

    .. image:: http://swharden.com/pyabf/graphics/action-potentials-small.png
        :alt: pyABF electrophysiology data analysis with Python and Matplotlib
        :align: center
```

# Quickstart

Install or upgrade pyABF:

```bash
pip install --upgrade pyabf
```

Access ABF sweep data:

```python
import pyabf
abf = pyabf.ABF("demo.abf")
abf.setSweep(sweepNumber=3, channel=0)
print(abf.sweepY) # displays sweep data (ADC)
print(abf.sweepX) # displays sweep times (seconds)
print(abf.sweepC) # displays command waveform (DAC)
```

Plot a sweep with Matplotlib:

```python
import pyabf
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
abf = pyabf.ABF("demo.abf")
abf.setSweep(14)
plt.plot(abf.sweepX, abf.sweepY)
plt.show()
```

```
.. class:: no-web

    .. image:: http://swharden.com/pyabf/graphics/pyabf-example-sweep.jpg
        :alt: pyABF Example
        :align: center
```

# Additional Examples

Full pyabf API documentation, additional code examples, a pyabf cookbook,
and low-level information about the ABF file format can be found at the pyABF
project homepage: <http://swharden.com/pyabf/>

```
.. class:: no-web

    .. image:: http://swharden.com/pyabf/graphics/pyabf-example-action-potentials.jpg
        :alt: pyABF Example
        :align: center
```

modules.name: pyabf.waveform
documentation:
Code here relates to the creation of stimulus waveforms from epoch tables.

In the waveform editor of ClampEx, you can edit the stimulus waveform table
for each channel (DAC). Each waveform table is made of epochs, which appear
as a single column of values in the waveform editor.

The overall strategy here is that data for each epoch (column) is stored in
an Epoch object. A single channel (DAC) has a list of Epoch objects, and can
be considered an epoch table. These epoch tables (Epoch lists) can be passed
around to be displayed or used to generate signal waveforms.

The epoch table must be built and stepped through (sweep by sweep) before an
epoch waveform can be created for a single sweep. This is because levels for
one sweep can depend on the last level of the previous sweep.

The minimum amount of information actually needed to create a waveform for a
sweep is: level, levelDelta, duration, durationDelta, pulsePeriod, pulseWidth,
and epochType. The EpochSweepWaveform holds this minimum amount of information
for every sweep, and contains the code necessary to generate waveforms from it.

functions.name: pyabf.waveform._demo_create_graphs
documentation: Plot sweepC of ABFs containing waveforms of all types.

functions.name: pyabf.waveform._demo_epoch_table
documentation: Demonstrate the text epoch table feature.

functions.name: pyabf.waveform._demo_epoch_access
documentation: Demonstrate how to access epoch levels and point indexes.

functions.name: pyabf.waveform._demo_sweepC
documentation: Demonstrate how to generate the sweepC waveform.

functions.name: pyabf.waveform._demo_digOut_by_channel
documentation: Demonstrate that only a single channel commands digital outputs.

functions.name: pyabf.waveform._demo_sweepD
documentation: Demonstrate how to access digital outputs.

functions.name: pyabf.waveform._demo_deltaT
documentation: Demonstrate delta duration.

classes.name: pyabf.waveform.Epoch
documentation:
The Epoch class represents data contained in a single epoch column
of the stimulus waveform editor. It's just an easy way to hold and convert
all the data values for a single epoch.

methods.name: pyabf.waveform.EpochSweepWaveform.__init__
documentation:
This object holds the minimal amount of information needed to create
and retrieve information about a sweep's epoch waveform. This object
can only be populated after the full waveform table is created and
stepped through sweep-by-sweep (because levels in a sweep can depend
on levels of the previous sweep).

Waveform synthesis happens in this class.

methods.name: pyabf.waveform.EpochSweepWaveform.getDigitalWaveform
documentation: Return a waveform reflecting a certain digital output channel.

methods.name: pyabf.waveform.EpochTable.__init__
documentation:
An epoch table represents all epochs for a single DAC.
Upon instantiation, epoch waveform objects are created for every sweep.

methods.name: pyabf.waveform.EpochTable._initABF1
documentation: Populate epoch values from the ABF1 header.

methods.name: pyabf.waveform.EpochTable._initABF2
documentation: Populate epoch values from the ABF2 header.

methods.name: pyabf.waveform.EpochTable._valToBitList
documentation:
Given an integer, return a list of 0s and 1s representing the state of
each of the bit.

methods.name: pyabf.waveform.EpochTable.text
documentation:
Given a list of epochs, return a text string formatted to look like the
epoch table displayed as plain text in pCLAMP and ClampFit.

methods.name: pyabf.waveform.EpochTable.getEpochWaveformsBySweep
documentation: Return a list of EpochSweepWaveform objects (one per sweep).

classes.name: pyabf.abf1.headerV1.HeaderV1
documentation:
The first several bytes of an ABF1 file contain variables
located at specific byte positions from the start of the file.
All ABF1 header values are read in this single block.
Arrays which reference ADC entries are shown as read, no physical <-> logical
channel mapping and interpretation of the sampling sequence is done.

functions.name: pyabf.names.getUserListParameterName
documentation:
Given an epoch paramater number return a human readable description
of what it's supposed to modify in the epoch table.
Based on: ABFHEADR.H#L530-L549

modules.name: pyabf.filter
documentation:
Code here relates to filering of ABF data.
Usually this means low-pass filtering to reduce noise.

functions.name: pyabf.filter._kernelGaussian
documentation:
Return a 1d array shaped like a Gaussian curve with area of 1.
Optionally provide a sigma (the larger, the wider the curve).

functions.name: pyabf.filter._convolve
documentation:
Convolve the data with the kernel. The edges of the returned data (half the
size of the kernel) will be nan. If you want a different convolution method,
code it yourself!

functions.name: pyabf.filter.remove
documentation:
Revert to the original data in the ABF. This is accomplished by opening
the original file and re-reading the data (into abf.data).

functions.name: pyabf.filter.gaussian
documentation:
Perform a gaussian convolution on every sweep of the indicated channel.
Note that this performs smoothing once (acting directly on abf.data), and
subsequent calls will keep smoothing the smoothed trace.

Set sigmaMs to 0 or False to remove the filter.

functions.name: pyabf.filter._test_01_different_sigmas
documentation: Show how the same ephys trace looks when filtered with different sigmas.

classes.name: pyabf.abf2.tagSection.TagSection
documentation:
Tags are comments placed in ABF files during the recording. Physically
they are located at the end of the file (after the data).

Later we will populate the times and sweeps (human-understandable units)
by multiplying the lTagTime by fSynchTimeUnit from the protocol section.

classes.name: pyabf.abf2.userListSection.UserListSection
documentation:
Contains elements of the ABF2 user list.
The user list allows custom values to be used as part of the epoch table.

classes.name: pyabf.abf2.synchArraySection.SynchArraySection
documentation:
Contains start time (in fSynchTimeUnit units) and length (in 
multiplexed samples) of each portion of the data if the data 
are not part of a continuous gap-free acquisition.

classes.name: pyabf.abf2.protocolSection.ProtocolSection
documentation:
This section contains information about the recording settings.
This is useful for determining things like sample rate and
channel scaling factors.

classes.name: pyabf.abf2.epochPerDacSection.EpochPerDACSection
documentation:
This section contains waveform protocol information. These are most of
the values set when using the epoch the waveform editor. Note that digital
output signals are not stored here, but are in EpochSection.

classes.name: pyabf.abf2.dacSection.DACSection
documentation:
Information about the DAC (what gets clamped).
There is 1 item per DAC.

classes.name: pyabf.abf2.stringsSection.StringsSection
documentation:
Part of the ABF file contains long strings. Some of these can be broken
apart into indexed strings.

The first string is the only one which seems to contain useful information.
This contains information like channel names, channel units, and abf
protocol path and comments. The other strings are very large and I do not
know what they do.

Strings which contain indexed substrings are separated by \x00 characters.

classes.name: pyabf.abf2.headerV2.HeaderV2
documentation:
The first several bytes of an ABF2 file contain variables
located at specific byte positions from the start of the file.

classes.name: pyabf.abf2.adcSection.ADCSection
documentation:
Information about the ADC (what gets recorded).
There is 1 item per ADC.

modules.name: pyabf.abf
documentation:
Code here provides direct access to the header and signal data of ABF files.
Efforts are invested to ensure ABF1 and ABF2 files are supported identically.

This file is LIMITED TO THE MANAGEMENT OF HEADER AND DATA information.
Analysis routines are not written in the ABF class itself. If useful, they
are to be written in another file and imported as necessary.

classes.name: pyabf.abf.ABF

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF.__init__
documentation:
Load header and sweep data from an ABF file.

### Parameters

1. abfFilePath -- path to the ABF file.

2. loadData -- whether or not to load sweep data values from the file immediately on instantiation.
Set this to False if you intent to iterate many ABF files rapidly and only inspect their headers.

3. cacheStimulusFiles -- Some ABF files use a source ABF as a stimulus file to control its DAC.
This module automatically loads the stimulus waveform from disk if it is available.
This parameter controls whether stimulus files should be stored in memory at the module level.
Set to False to prevent memory issues if you intend to iterate very large numbers of ABFs with
unique stimulus waveforms.

4. stimulusFileFolder -- Some ABF files use a source ABF as a stimulus file to control its DAC.
This module automatically loads the stimulus waveform from disk if it is available.
The ABF header contains an absolute file path to the stimulus file used to control the DAC.
If supplied, this path is used as an alternate search path to look for stimulus files with the same filename
in the case the original path does not exist on the machine loading the ABF.

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF.__str__
documentation: Return a string describing basic properties of the loaded ABF.

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF._readHeadersV1
documentation: Populate class variables from the ABF1 header.

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF._readHeadersV2
documentation: Populate class variables from the ABF2 header.

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF._makeAdditionalVariables
documentation: create or touch-up version-nonspecific variables.

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF._loadAndScaleData
documentation: Load data from the ABF file and scale it by its scaleFactor.

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF._ide_helper
documentation:
Add things here to help auto-complete IDEs aware of things added by
external modules. This function should never actually get called.

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF.headerText
documentation: Return all header information as a text-formatted string.

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF.headerMarkdown
documentation: Return all header information as a markdown-formatted string.

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF.headerHTML
documentation: Return all header information as a text-formatted string.

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF.headerLaunch
documentation: Display ABF header information in the web browser.

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF.saveABF1
documentation:
Save this ABF file as an ABF1 file compatible with ClampFit and MiniAnalysis.
Not all header values are saved, but the minimum necessary are to read sweep data.

### Parameters
1. filePath -- path of the ABF1 file to create

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF.launchInClampFit
documentation:
Launch the ABF in the default ABF viewing program (usually ClampFit) as
if it were double-clicked in the windows explorer. 

This assumes ClampFit is installed on your system and the default application
for opening ABF files.

This will fail if ClampFit is already open.

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF.setSweep
documentation:
Args:

### Parameters
1. sweepNumber -- sweep number to load (starting at 0)
2. channel -- ABF channel (starting at 0)
3. absoluteTime -- Whether sweepX should represent time in sweep or time in file.
    If False, the first value of sweepX will always be 0.
    If True, sweepX of successive sweeps will start at larger and larger values.
4. baseline -- If two times are provided (in seconds), the entire sweep will be subtracted
    to the mean of the sweep between those two times (baseline subtraction).
    Use [None, None] to disable automatic baseline subtraction.

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF.sweepC
documentation: Generate the sweep command waveform.

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF.sweepC
documentation:
Manually define sweepC so the given sweepData will always be returned as
sweepC and the stimulus waveform will no longer be automatically generated
or loaded from file. Undo this by deleting "abf._sweepC".

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF.sweepD
documentation: Generate a waveform for the given digital output.

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF.sweepTimesSec
documentation: Numpy array of sweep start times (in seconds)

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF.sweepTimesMin
documentation: Numpy array of sweep start times (in minutes)

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF.sweepDerivative
documentation: First derivative of sweepY (delta units / second)

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF.md5
documentation: MD5 hash string of the whole ABF file.

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF.fileUUID
documentation: Create a unique ABF file ID using the MD5 of the whole file.

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF.sampleRate
documentation: Sample rate (Hz) for each channel

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF.getAllYs
documentation: Return data from all sweeps for the specified channel.

methods.name: pyabf.abf.ABF.getAllXs
documentation: Return times from all sweeps for the specified channel.

The ABF class provides direct access to the header and signal data of ABF
files. It can load ABF1 and ABF2 files identically.

The default action is to read all the ABF data from disk when the class is
instantiated. When disabled (with an argument) to save speed, one can
quickly iterate through many ABF files to access header contents.

Although you can access all data with abf.data, you can also call
abf.setSweep() then access abf.sweepX and abf.sweepY and similar values.

classes.name: pyabf.abf2.epochSection.EpochSection
documentation:
This section contains the digital output signals for each epoch. This
section has been overlooked by some previous open-source ABF-reading
projects. Note that the digital output is a single byte, but represents
8 bits corresponding to 8 outputs (7->0). When working with these bits,
I convert it to a string like "10011101" for easy eyeballing.

modules.name: pyabf
documentation:
pyABF - A Python interface to files in the Axon Binary Format (ABF)
    by Scott Harden

Documentation and code examples, and more can be found at:
    https://github.com/swharden/pyABF

functions.name: pyabf.info
documentation: display information about the pyabf package.

functions.name: pyabf.help
documentation: launch the pyABF project page in a browser.

modules.name: pyabf.stimulus
documentation:
ABFs can be created when signals are applied using the DAC. If these "stimulus
waveforms" are used, they either come from an epoch table or from a DAC file.
Code in this file determines where the stimulus comes from and returns it for
a given sweep and channel.

If the stimulus waveform comes from a file, code here also assists in caching
the data from that file so the file only needs to be read from disk once.

functions.name: pyabf.stimulus.findStimulusWaveformFile
documentation:
Look for the stimulus waveform file in several places. Return the path
where it can be found. Return None if it cannot be found.

The original path is an absolute windows filename stored in the ABF header.

functions.name: pyabf.stimulus.stimulusWaveformFromFile
documentation:
Attempt to find the stimulus file used to record an ABF, read the stimulus
file (ABF or ATF), and return the stimulus waveform (as a numpy array).

classes.name: pyabf.stimulus.Stimulus

methods.name: pyabf.stimulus.Stimulus.stimulusWaveform
documentation:
Return a signal (the same size as a sweep) representing the command
waveform of the DAC for the given channel.

The Stimulus class used to be where all waveform generation happened.
Waveform generation from the epoch table now occurs in waveform.py.
This class is kept so old code doesn't break, but is getting dismantled.

modules.name: pyabf.tools.ap
documentation: Code related to detection and measurement of action potentials (APs)

functions.name: pyabf.tools.ap._where_cross
documentation: return a list of Is where the data first crosses above threshold.

functions.name: pyabf.tools.ap.ap_points_currentSweep
documentation: Primitive AP detection. Returns index numbers of peaks of ap depolariztaion velocities.

functions.name: pyabf.tools.ap.ap_freq_per_sweep
documentation: Return [apFreqInBin, apFreqFirst] lists by sweep.

modules.name: pyabf.tools.memtestMath
documentation:
This file contains methods which calculate passive membrane properties from
voltage-clamp sweeps. These methods are not optimized for speed or accuracy
and are only provided for backwards compatibility. pyabf is intended to be
used just for reading ABF files, and users are encouraged to write their own
python code to perform analysis of ABF data.

This URL has useful discussion and code for calculating membrane properties:
https://swharden.com/blog/2020-10-11-model-neuron-ltspice

functions.name: pyabf.tools.memtestMath._cm_ramp_points_and_voltages
documentation:
Return [points, voltages] if the sweep contains a ramp suitable for 
capacitance calculation using a matching doward and upward ramp.
points is a list of 3 numbers depicting index values important to this
ramp. The first number is the index at the start of the downward ramp, the
second is the index of its nadir, and the third is the index where it
returns to the original level.
voltages is a list of 2 numbers: voltage before and during the ramp.

functions.name: pyabf.tools.memtestMath.currentSweepRamp
documentation:
Calculate capacitance from a voltage clamp ramp.
In theory any channel, any ramp, and even changing ramps will work.
This expects a downward ramp followed by an upward ramp, each with the same
duration and magnitude. This ramp can be anywhere in the sweep, and does
not have to be the first epoch.

functions.name: pyabf.tools.memtestMath._cm_ramp_calculate
documentation:
Given a v-shaped current trace in response to a symmetric voltage-clamp 
ramp (its magnitude given by deltaVoltage), calculate and return the
capacitance of the cell. Data units must be in pA, sampleRate in points/sec,
and voltage in mV. Positive (upward) or negative (V-shaped) ramps are okay.
centerFrac is the fractional time span to draw data from in the center of
each ramp.

functions.name: pyabf.tools.memtestMath._tauMonoExpFit
documentation:
Given some data which decays to zero, return its time constant 
(found by successive approximation) in seconds.

functions.name: pyabf.tools.memtestMath.currentSweepStep
documentation: Returns [Ih, Rm, Ra, Cm] from a step protocol of the current sweep.

functions.name: pyabf.tools.memtestMath._step_calculate
documentation:
Given a current trace depicting the resposne to a hyperpolarizing square 
pulse voltage step, return membrane test information Ih, Rm, Ra, and Cm.
A typical use is to give a trace which starts clamped at -70 mV, then gets
clamped to -80 mV. The traceStepPoint is the index value at which the
transition between -70 and -80 occurs.
stepAvgLastFrac - defines what percentage of the end of the step to measure
to calculate resting current.
dV - step voltage change (mV)

functions.name: pyabf.tools.memtestMath._step_points_and_voltages
documentation:
Return [stepPoints,stepVoltages] if the sweep contains a step suitable for 
capacitance calculation using the first square step from holding command.
stepPoints is a list of 3 numbers depicting index values important to this
step. Typically the first number is the index of the trace clamped at -70, 
the second number is index of the trace where it gets clamped to -80, and
the final number is the index after settling at -80 for a while.
stepVoltages is a list of 2 numbers: voltage before and during the step.

modules.name: pyabf.tools.memtest
documentation:
Code here relates to the detection and management of cell membrane properties:
  * holding current (Ih)
  * access resistance (Ra)
  * membrane resistance (Rm)
  * membrane capacitance (Cm)

This modules provides two types of membrane capacitance values: ones derived
from step protocols and ones derived from ramp protocols.

This URL has useful discussion and code for calculating membrane properties:
https://swharden.com/blog/2020-10-11-model-neuron-ltspice

methods.name: pyabf.tools.memtest.Memtest.__init__
documentation:
This object contains passive cell membrane properties calculated from 
each sweep an episodic protocol containing a voltage-clamp step.

WARNING: These calculations are not optimized for speed or accuracy. 
This module is experimental and included only for backward compatibility
purposes. Users are highly encouraged to write their own analysis code.

modules.name: pyabf.tools.sweep
documentation: Code here relates to measurements performed on ABF sweeps.

classes.name: pyabf.tools.sweep.SweepMeasurement
documentation:
This data transfer object represents a single metric measured once per sweep.
This is intended to be used for by-sweep means for memtest and event detection.

modules.name: pyabf.tools.generate
documentation:
Code here relates to the from-scratch creation of data which simulates
electrical recordings of neurons. Creating data from scratch is useful 
because (1) you can generate your own ABFs to analyze without requiring
access to real recordings and, (2) generating data with known events allows you
to test your event detection code to ensure the results are what you expect.
Same goes for simulating membrane tests, then analyzing them.

functions.name: pyabf.tools.generate.generate_exp
documentation:
Create a signal similar to an EPSC or IPSC with a sharp rise and 
exponential decay. Apply a Gaussian convolution (to simulate the low-pass
effect of Cm/Ra) to take the sharp edge off the rising slope.

functions.name: pyabf.tools.generate.generate_alpha
documentation: Create a signal similar to an EPSP or IPSP with a slow rise and decay.

functions.name: pyabf.tools.generate._kernel_gaussian
documentation:
Return a 1d array shaped like a Gaussian curve with area of 1.
Optionally provide a sigma (the larger, the wider the curve).

functions.name: pyabf.tools.generate._demo_EPSCs_IPSCs
documentation: Create a lifelike sweep containing sEPSCs and sIPSCs.

functions.name: pyabf.tools.generate._demo_EPSPs_IPSPs
documentation: Create a lifelike sweep containing sEPSPs and sIPSPs.

methods.name: pyabf.tools.generate.SynthSweep.addOffset
documentation: Add a fixed offset (pA) to the trace.

methods.name: pyabf.tools.generate.SynthSweep.addNoise
documentation: Add normal (gaussian) noise to the trace.

methods.name: pyabf.tools.generate.SynthSweep.addWobble
documentation: Add a random walk to simulate slight instability.

methods.name: pyabf.tools.generate.SynthSweep.addEvent
documentation: Add a single post-synaptic event at a given time point.

methods.name: pyabf.tools.generate.SynthSweep.addEvents
documentation: Add stochastic spontaneous post-synaptic currents or potentials.

methods.name: pyabf.tools.generate.SynthSweep.addGlutamate
documentation: Add spontaneous excitatory post-synaptic currents or potentials.

methods.name: pyabf.tools.generate.SynthSweep.addGABA
documentation: Add spontaneous inhibitory post-synaptic currents or potentials.

methods.name: pyabf.tools.generate.SynthSweep.addAPs
documentation: Add spontaneous action potentials (Poisson distributed).

methods.name: pyabf.tools.generate.SynthSweep.plot
documentation: Display the current sweep.

modules.name: pyabf.tools.abfHeaderDisplay
documentation:
Code here relates to text parsing and creation of HTML and markdown files
to display the ABF header in a neat and organized way.

functions.name: pyabf.tools.abfHeaderDisplay.standardNumpyText
documentation: return a numpy array as a standard string regardless of numpy version

functions.name: pyabf.tools.abfHeaderDisplay.abfInfoPage
documentation:
Return an object to let the user inspect methods and variables
of this ABF class as well as the full contents of the ABF header

classes.name: pyabf.tools.abfHeaderDisplay.InfoPage

methods.name: pyabf.tools.abfHeaderDisplay.InfoPage.getText
documentation: Return information about all objects as markdown-formatted text.

The InfoPage class is designed to hold information about
python objects in such a way that it can be easily viewed.
Build the page with sections, docs, and things, then
get it as HTML, markdown, or text.

modules.name: pyabf.abfWriter
documentation:
Code here relates to modification and de-novo creation of ABF files.
Files are saved as ABF1 format ABFs, which are easy to create because their
headers are simpler than ABF2 files. 

Many values (e.g., epoch waveform table) are left blank, so when they are read 
by an ABF reader their values may not make sense (especially when converted to 
floating-point numbers).

functions.name: pyabf.abfWriter.writeABF1
documentation:
Create an ABF1 file from scratch and write it to disk.
Files created with this function are compatible with MiniAnalysis.
Data is expected to be a 2D numpy array (each row is a sweep).

modules.name: pyabf.atf
documentation:
This file contains code simplifying access to data in ATF files.

One goal of this module is to produce an ATF class with virtually all the same
methods as the ABF class.

ATF file format description:
https://mdc.custhelp.com/app/answers/detail/a_id/18883/~/genepix%C2%AE-file-formats

classes.name: pyabf.atf.ATF

methods.name: pyabf.atf.ATF.__init__
documentation:
Load header and sweep data from an ATF file.

### Parameters
1. atfFilePath -- path to the ATF file
2. loadData -- whether or not to parse sweep data values on instantiation

methods.name: pyabf.atf.ATF.setSweep
documentation:
Update the ATF class to hold data, labels, and units for the given sweep.

### Parameters
1. sweepNumber -- zero-indexed sweep number
2. channel -- zero-indexed channel number

The ATF class provides simplistic access to the header and data which exist 
in Axon Text Format (ATF) files. This class supports the reading of multi-
channel data and has a setSweep() function similar to the ABF class.

methods.name: pyabf.abfReader.AbfReader.readStruct
documentation:
Return a structured value in an ABF file as a Python object.
If cleanStrings is enabled, ascii-safe strings are returned.

methods.name: pyabf.abfReader.AbfReader.readStruct
documentation:
Return a structured value in an ABF file as a Python object.
If cleanStrings is enabled, ascii-safe strings are returned.

modules.name: pyabf.plot
documentation:
This file contains code to plot ABF data using matplotlib.

Users are typically encouraged to write their own plotting functions, but
I have added common tasks here to serve as a reference and potentially even
inspiration (for those who have not used matplotlib before).

Code in this module is mostly for testing and it is not actively developed.

functions.name: pyabf.plot.sweepDataRange
documentation:
Return the magnitude of the range between the min and max points in the
sweep. This is useful when determining how much to stack an ABF.

functions.name: pyabf.plot.colorsBinned
documentation:
Return a list of colors spanning the range of the given colormap.
I like using these colormaps: Winter, Dark2, rainbow, viridis

functions.name: pyabf.plot.sweeps
documentation:
This is a flexible sweep plotting function. Although it has many potential 
uses, developers will most likely want to write their own plotting functions
to suit their specific applications.

functions.name: pyabf.plot.scalebar
documentation:
Add an L-shaped scalebar to the current figure.
This removes current axis labels, ticks, and the figure frame.
